did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Artificial Neural Network (specifically a Two-Layer neural network) to perform multi-class diagnosis of ten types of skin lesions (including Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, etc.) based on Fourier spectral information extracted from RGB images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a new methodology for multi-class diagnosis of skin lesions using Fourier spectral information and an Artificial Neural Network. It details the methodology, database (Edinburgh Dermofit Library), results (Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Specificity metrics), and conclusions, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multi-Class Diagnosis of Skin Lesions Using the Fourier Spectral Information of Images on Additive Color Model by Artificial Neural Network
brief_summary: This study proposes a methodology for the multi-class diagnosis of ten types of skin lesions using Fourier spectral information extracted from RGB images via an additive color model. The input signals for a Two-Layer Artificial Neural Network consisted of 26 parameters, including 16 spectral features and 10 correlation coefficients derived from the Edinburgh Dermofit Library dataset. The model was evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, and Specificity metrics across Training, Validation, and Testing sub-datasets. The proposed method achieved high overall performance with Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, and Specificity values of 99.33%, 94.16%, 92.9%, and 99.63%, respectively. Basal Cell Carcinoma, Seborrhoeic Keratosis, and Melanocytic Nevus exhibited the best classification performance, while Pyogenic Granuloma showed the worst results.

all_task_types.target: Ten discrete skin lesion classes (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Seborrhoeic Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevus, Pyogenic Granuloma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Hemangioma, Actinic Keratosis, Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions using a Two-Layer Artificial Neural Network. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories based on Fourier spectral information. Performance was evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Specificity, and ROC analysis metrics.

datasets_used.name: Edinburgh Dermofit Library
datasets_used.description: A public image database of skin lesions used as the source for the study's dataset. The images were obtained from the Edinburgh Dermofit Library website.

use.training: Used to generate input signals (Fourier spectral features and correlation coefficients) for the Artificial Neural Network. The paper mentions a Training sub-dataset was used with a misclassification rate of approximately 2.8%.
use.validation: Used to generate input signals for the Artificial Neural Network. The paper mentions a Validation sub-dataset was used with a misclassification rate of approximately 5.6%.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance. The paper mentions a Testing sub-dataset was used with a misclassification rate of approximately 5.9%.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate correlation coefficients between the features vector of each particular sample and the general correlation vector for each class.

To generate performance metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Specificity) and calculate True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), False Negatives (FN), and True Negatives (TN) for classification evaluation.
To determine if classification results are relevant by analyzing the relationship between True Positive Rate (TPR) and False Positive Rate (FPR).

statistical_methods_used.method: Slope (m) and PROC (magnitude of numerical pair) analysis
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification quality and relevance, where m = TPR/FPR and PROC = sqrt(FPR^2 + TPR^2), serving as an alternative to conventional ROC representation.
To evaluate the training stage performance of the neural network, with values indicating convergence towards zero for perfect classification.
To quantify the variability in classification performance across 40 algorithm runs for each class.
To aggregate performance metrics (e.g., correct classification percentage) across 40 algorithm runs for each class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean, standard deviation) and classification metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Specificity, ROC analysis) across 40 algorithm runs.

Training Sub-dataset.notes: Cross-entropy mean value was ≈5.21. Class 8 (ALLBCC) had the best performance with 99.23% correct classification.

Validation Sub-dataset.notes: Cross-entropy mean value was ≈14.8. Class 6 (PYO) had the worst performance with 59.4% correct classification.

Testing Sub-dataset.notes: Cross-entropy mean value was ≈14.9. Class 6 (PYO) had the worst performance.

Overall Performance (Predictors Rule 1).notes: Class 6 (PYO) had the lowest Sensitivity (69.56 ± 4.34%). Classes 8 (ALLBCC), 9 (ALLSK), and 10 (ALLML) had the best ROC performance. Class 10 (ALLML) had the best slope (m=9894) and PROC (0.9894) values.

Comparison with Predictors Rule 2.notes: PR1 showed higher correct classification percentages and lower standard deviation compared to PR2. PR1 is considered better for classification.

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping: Images were cropped at the center to focus on the skin lesion spot and eliminate surrounding context.
Dimension normalization: The method accounts for images with different dimensions (pixel per inch, brightness, orientation) by processing both original and cropped images.
Color space conversion: Images were processed in RGB format and decomposed into individual R, G, and B components for spectral analysis.
Fourier Transform: 2-D Fourier Transform was applied to original and cropped images to extract magnitude and phase spectral information.
Feature vector construction: A features vector was created for each image based on spectral indicators from original and cropped images.
Correlation coefficient calculation: Pearson’s correlation coefficients were calculated between sample feature vectors and general class correlation vectors.
conclusions: The proposed methodology using Fourier spectral information and a Two-Layer Artificial Neural Network achieved high overall performance for multi-class diagnosis of ten skin lesion types, with Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, and Specificity values of 99.33%, 94.16%, 92.9%, and 99.63%, respectively.
The method demonstrated robustness by maintaining high classification accuracy despite image quality issues such as strange objects, lack of clarity, and black sections in some images.
Basal Cell Carcinoma, Seborrhoeic Keratosis, and Melanocytic Nevus exhibited the best classification performance, particularly regarding Receiver Operating Characteristics (ROC) metrics.
Pyogenic Granuloma showed the worst classification performance among the studied classes, with the lowest Sensitivity (69.56 ± 4.34%).

The 'Predictors Rule 1' (using 26 parameters including correlation coefficients) provided significantly better classification performance and lower standard deviation compared to 'Predictors Rule 2' (using 48 parameters).
The proposed method allows for the classification of a higher number of classes (ten) compared to some prior methods, while achieving better performance than previously reported results.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public dataset (Edinburgh Dermofit Library) without external validation, limiting generalizability to other clinical populations or imaging devices.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Melanocytic Nevus (331 images) and Seborrhoeic Keratosis (257 images) being overrepresented compared to other classes, potentially biasing the model.
Pyogenic Granuloma (Class 6) showed the worst classification performance with the lowest Sensitivity (69.56 ± 4.34%), indicating poor detection capability for this specific lesion type.

The model achieved high overall metrics (Accuracy 99.33%, Specificity 99.63%) but lower Precision (94.16%) and Sensitivity (92.9%), suggesting a potential trade-off between false positives and false negatives.
The methodology uses a simple Two-Layer Artificial Neural Network rather than deep learning architectures, which may limit the complexity of features captured compared to state-of-the-art CNNs.

The paper acknowledges image irregularities such as strange objects, lack of clarity, and black sections in the source images, yet claims robustness; however, no specific preprocessing to remove these artifacts is detailed beyond center cropping.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; results are based on descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) from 40 algorithm runs.

The study compares two predictor rules, finding that Predictors Rule 1 (26 parameters) significantly outperformed Predictors Rule 2 (48 parameters), highlighting the importance of feature selection over quantity in this context.
